div.p: To all sons of holy Mother Church, Roger Avenel, son of Gervase Avenel, sends greeting. Know me to have granted and by this my charter to have confirmed to God and the church of Saint Mary at Melrose, and to the Monks serving God in that place, the donation of Robert Avenel, my grandfather, which Gervase Avenel, my father, confirmed to them; that is, the land of Eskdale which the aforesiad Robert Avenel, my grandfather, donated to them in everlasting alms, the most pious King Malcolm first approving, and afterwards the illustrious King William, his brother; to wit, Tomleuchar and Watcarrick, in free, pure, and everlasting alms; for the soul of the glorious King David, who had donated the same land to my same [grandfather] [in copying from the earlier charter, the scribe originally wrote 'patri', but erased it, forgetting to insert 'auo'] in return for his service; and for the soul of Earl Henry and of the aforesaid King Malcolm, and for the soul of the said King William and of Alexander, the son of the king of Scots, my lord, and of all his ancestors' and successors' [souls], and for my own soul and of all my ancestors' and successors' [souls]. I have therefore granted this, to wit, Tomleuchar and Watcarrik, to the aforesaid monks, and have given and confirmed and described in a written deed by this my charter, by these boundaries, namely, as the two Esks meet, so upwards by the Black Esk as far as the first stream that descends from Hartside; and from that stream, upwards through the middle ridge of the mountains, between them and Robert de Brus, as the waters descend towards the monks themselves; and so between the forest of Timeus and themselves, by the middle ridge of the mountains, as they descend towards themselves; similarly between the land of Borthwick and the monks themselves, by the middle ridge of the mountains, as the waters descend towards them; and so as far as the land of Hawick, and thence across by the middle ridge of the mountains which is between myself and the aforesaid monks, as the waters descend towards them; this ridge encloses Harwde towards the monks, and so descends from the east side from Harwde itself, and so always descending to where the two Esks meet.And this whole aforenamed land, possession, and whatever is contained within these aforesaid boundaries, in wood and plain, in meadows and pastures, in waters and marshes, and in all benefits and easements, I have granted and given, and made firm to the aforesaid monks, in pure and everlasting alms, to be held and possessed by perpetual right, free and quit from me and my heirs, and released from all services, and from every custom and secular exaction, as freely and quitely, fully and honourably, as any alms is able to be held and possessed. And if by chance anyone should wish to claim the aforesaid alms, I and my heirs shall answer all claimants, and sufficiently protect and warrant it for the aforesaid monks. This is also to be known: that I have retained for myself and my heirs only deer and hind, swine and roebuck, within the aforenamed boundaries, without harm and injury of crops, meadows, hedges, livestock, animals, and all the other possessions of the same monks. And although I and my heirs shall not have a forester within the described boundaries, nevertheless we shall have within the same boundaries robbers, and eyries of hawks and sparrow-hawks, and the guards to guard them, and trespassers; and it should be known that the aforesaid monks shall have and possess the whole aforesaid land, free and quit from me and my heirs, saving my aforesaid wild game, and the eyries of hawks and sparrow-hawks; and the monks shall make use of all this tenement according to their will and pleasure, just as it shall seem to them best and most useful to be of use to themselves, their brethren, their servants and livestock. With the exception that they shall not hunt there with packs of hounds and ropes, nor bring others there for hunting, nor set traps there, unless for catching wolves; and they shall not take the eyries of hawks and sparrow-hawks within these boundaries. It should be known in addition that the five merks which at one time my grandfather received in ferme from them, which ... his wife and also my father quitclaimed to them before his death; this I likewise have quitclaimed and given to them in alms and have remitted for ever, and this same ... I have confirmed by charter. And that this alms should be established and firm for ever, nor could by any means be declared invalid, the lord William, king of Scots, by [my] petition and request, has granted and given [this] alms to them, and confirmed by his charter. As witnesses, Richard, abbot of Kelso, Walter Oliphant, William son of John, John of Maxwell, Bernard of Howden, Roger Burnard, Gregory of Rutherford, William of Cresswell, William the parson, my brother, and many others.
